questions.question: According to research cited in the lesson, what is one of the biggest course-development risks when teaching legacy work?

options.text: Not including enough practical exercises
Adopting an individualistic, achievement-oriented view that misfits many participants and overpathologizes cultural modesty, privacy, or relational contribution
Making the course too long and complex
Focusing too much on emotional content

questions.explanation: Research identifies this as one of the biggest risks: If legacy work adopts an individualistic, achievement-oriented view (common in Western cultures), it may misfit many participants from collectivist or Indigenous cultures and overpathologize culturally normative forms of modesty, privacy, or relational contribution. 'Legacy' is not culturally neutral—it means different things across cultures. In some contexts, legacy means transmitting family stories, preserving rituals, caregiving, or maintaining continuity rather than leaving a public mark or achieving individual recognition.

questions.question: What is 'cultural humility' as defined in the lesson?

options.text: Mastering knowledge about all cultures through training
An ongoing learning stance: 'I don't know your culture's meanings. Teach me.' Recognition that you're always learning, never arrived at full understanding
Treating everyone the same regardless of cultural background
Avoiding all cross-cultural interaction for fear of getting it wrong

questions.explanation: Cultural humility is an ongoing learning stance, not claiming expertise: 'I don't know your culture's meanings. Teach me.' It involves recognizing you're always learning (never fully arrived), acknowledging your own cultural lens, asking rather than assuming, inviting correction, and adapting based on feedback. This is different from 'cultural competence' (which implies you've mastered a culture), different from colorblindness (which erases real differences), and not the same as paralysis/avoidance. It's active, humble engagement with cultural difference.

questions.question: Which of the following is identified as a UNIVERSAL theme that appears across cultures (though expressed differently)?

options.text: Individual achievement and public recognition
Written memoirs and documentation
Care for the next generation (generativity) - though HOW this care is expressed varies by culture
Social media presence and digital legacy

questions.explanation: Generativity (care for next generation) is identified as a universal theme—across cultures, humans care about what happens to the next generation (children, students, community's future). However, HOW this care is expressed varies enormously by culture. Western cultures may emphasize individual mentoring and leaving achievements; collectivist cultures may emphasize family continuity and tradition transmission; Indigenous cultures may emphasize seven-generation stewardship. The lesson's key insight: generativity is universal; specific practices are cultural. Don't assume your culture's generativity form is the only valid one.

questions.question: In the Cultural Adaptation Process, what is the recommended FIRST step before adapting legacy practices for other cultures?

options.text: Research all possible cultures you might encounter
Start with your own cultural location - name your culture's assumptions about aging, success, family, individualism before trying to see others' cultures
Create a single universal approach that works for everyone
Hire professional translators

questions.explanation: The first step in cultural adaptation is: Start with your own cultural location. Before adapting for others, name your own culture's assumptions about aging, success, family, individualism, time, privacy, achievement. Why? You can't see others' cultures clearly until you see your own. Your 'normal' is culturally specific, not universal. Only after recognizing your own cultural lens can you effectively learn about and adapt to other cultural contexts without unconsciously imposing your assumptions.

questions.question: According to the lesson, when is it appropriate to step back from cross-cultural wisdom-sharing work?

options.text: Never - you should always push through cultural discomfort
When you lack cultural knowledge, lived experience, language capacity, or community trust needed to work effectively with a specific cultural group
Only if you've received explicit complaints
After you've tried at least three times to make it work

questions.explanation: The lesson explicitly states: 'You don't have to be all things to all people. If you lack cultural knowledge, lived experience, language capacity, or community trust needed to work effectively with a specific cultural group—it's appropriate to acknowledge that and refer to culturally matched practitioners, not force yourself into that space. Cultural humility includes knowing when to step aside.' This demonstrates mature cultural humility—recognizing your limits and prioritizing community wellbeing over your desire to help. Sometimes the most culturally humble choice is stepping back.

title: Global Impact and Cross-Cultural Wisdom Exchange - Quiz
